coredata.dc:title: The synthesis of gold nanoclusters with high stability and their application in fluorometric detection for Hg2+ and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Ruyan
Su, Dongyue
Song, Ying
Sun, Peng
Mao, Baodong
Tian, Miaomiao
Chai, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Sensing Hg2+ is significant to protecting human health and environmental ecosystems, for its toxicity and genotoxicity. Here, highly stable fluorescent folic acid (FA)-protected Au nanoclusters (FA-AuNCs) were synthesized by optimizing the reactive parameters with high quantum yield of 34.7%. Main components of Au4L were confirmed by MALDI-TOF, and the electron-rich residues of FA shell enabled FA-AuNCs excellent photostability. FA-AuNCs exhibited sensitive response behavior to Hg2+ with a minimum detectability of 1.3 nM, and presented extreme effect to the detection of Hg2+ in real water. Notably, the cellular imaging and in-situ detection of Hg2+ in cells can be achieved visually. The high selectivity was attributed to the chemical bond formed between Au+ (4f145d10) and Hg2+ (4f145d10). And the internal filter effect and static quenching effect were proved triggering the quenching of FA-AuNCs. The ultra-stable FA-AuNCs provide a potential promising opportunity for the in-situ tracing Hg2+ from environmental and biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au nanoclusters
Fluorescent nanoprobe
Mercury
Quantitative detection
Living cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Specificity and sensitivity of FA-AuNCs towards Hg2+
Detection of Hg2+ in real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge support from NNSF of China ( 22171060 ), Harbin Normal University ( HSDSSCX2022-14 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence intensity of FA-AuNCs obtained at different temperatures (30–100) with time (0.5–32 h).

(a) The FT-IR of FA and FA-AuNCs, (b) The XPS of the Au 4f, (c) absorption, excitation and emission of FA-AuNCs (inset: images under day-light and UV-light), (d) emission with different excitation, (e) 3D contour map, (f) fluorescence decay spectrum.
(a) TEM and particle size histogram of FA-AuNCs (inset: HRTEM), (b) EDX energy spectrum and (c) MALDI-TOF mass spectra of FA-AuNCs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of FA-AuNCs with 50 μM of Hg2+ and other ions (inset: local magnification), (b) quenching ratio ((I0–I)/I0) of FA-AuNCs with Hg2+ and other ions coexisted with Hg2+, (c) corresponding images.

(a) Sensitivity of FA-AuNCs for Hg2+ (0–200 μM), (b) linear relationship between Hg2+ concentration (2.0–10.0 nM) and I0/I, (c) the 3D waterfall of emission, FL intensity and concentration of Hg2+, and corresponding images (left to right: 0, 0.002, 0.004, 0.006, 0.008, 0.01, 0.1, 10, 30, 50, 100, 200 μM) under (d) UV-light, (e) day-light.
Histogram of recovered Hg2+ concentrations in real water.

The confocal FL imaging of FA-AuNCs incubated with Hela cells for 2 h: (a) bright field imaging, (b) corresponding FL imaging, (c) sensing Hg2+ in Hela cells, and the corresponding images analyzed using optical filter were shown in the (d), (e) and (f).
(a) UV–vis of Hg2+ and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the prepared FA-AuNCs, (b) fluorescence decay spectra of FA-AuNCs and FA-AuNCs mixed with Hg2+.
(a) Strategy of synthesis and detection utility of FA-AuNCs, (b) process of fluorescence quenching with Hg2+.

head.ce:title: The synthesis of gold nanoclusters with high stability and their application in fluorometric detection for Hg2+ and cell imaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensing Hg2+ is significant to protecting human health and environmental ecosystems, for its toxicity and genotoxicity. Here, highly stable fluorescent folic acid (FA)-protected Au nanoclusters (FA-AuNCs) were synthesized by optimizing the reactive parameters with high quantum yield of 34.7%. Main components of Au4L were confirmed by MALDI-TOF, and the electron-rich residues of FA shell enabled FA-AuNCs excellent photostability. FA-AuNCs exhibited sensitive response behavior to Hg2+ with a minimum detectability of 1.3 nM, and presented extreme effect to the detection of Hg2+ in real water. Notably, the cellular imaging and in-situ detection of Hg2+ in cells can be achieved visually. The high selectivity was attributed to the chemical bond formed between Au+ (4f145d10) and Hg2+ (4f145d10). And the internal filter effect and static quenching effect were proved triggering the quenching of FA-AuNCs. The ultra-stable FA-AuNCs provide a potential promising opportunity for the in-situ tracing Hg2+ from environmental and biological samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Hg2+ has become essential in the past decades due to the global awareness of its high toxicity to the environment and human health [1]. Trace amounts of contaminated Hg2+ can cause damage to immune system, lungs, kidney and permanent brain lesion [2], due to their poor degradability and strong liquidity [3]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) stipulates 10 nM as the maximum allowable limit for inorganic mercury in drinking water [4], it is urgent for exploiting sensitive, rapid and convenient method for Hg2+ detection. Currently, traditional technologies including atomic fluorescence spectrometry [5], atomic absorption spectrophotometry [6] and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7] have been applied to recognize Hg2+. Some common defects such as expensive equipment, complex pretreatment, bureaucratic detection procedure have restricted their rapid on-site detection. To response these weaknesses, tremendous efforts have been developed and mostly focused on nanomaterials via colorimetric [8], fluorescent [9,10]]and electrochemiluminescence method [11], due to their unique properties caused by nanosize effect. Among them, the fluorescent nanoprobes [12] have been explored to simplify and improve the detection by facile operation and visual readout. In particular, fluorescent nanomaterials such as carbon dots [13], semiconductor quantum dots [14], and AuNCs [15] have been developed to monitor heavy metal with rapid response, good sensitivity and selectivity. Among them, the AuNCs can be regarded as one of most attractive materials for their ultrasmall size, unique structure, easy modification and low toxicity, tremendous efforts have been done in designing AuNCs probe for heavy metals and bio-imaging [16]. Furthermore, AuNCs have also been employed in cell imaging, labeling, sensing, and theragnostic in the cellular molecular level due to their good biocompatibility [17]. The sensitive and tunable physicochemical properties of the AuNCs open up broad prospects for progressive applications in various interdisciplinary fields. However, the novel strategy for designing and synthesizing Au NCs is still a great challenge, because most AuNCs still have disadvantages of poor stability and weak environmental tolerance [18]. In particular, the stability of water-soluble AuNCs is still not good enough, few AuNCs could sustain their size, structure, and fluorescence for months at room temperature or elevated temperature [19]. Besides the stability, the fluorescence of AuNCs is still comparatively low, and most of water-soluble AuNCs have quantum yield (QYs) of <15% [20], which constraining their practical sensing applications. The design and synthesis of AuNCs were substantially concerned with a focus on brightness and photostability as two major properties of interest in most fluorescence sensing imaging experiments. Therefore, the advancement of new strategies to synthesize Au NCs with excellent photostability, high QY, and nontoxicity is urgently needed in the field of detection. Currently, investigators are devoted to the development of stable and highly luminescent AuNCs for sensor construction by modulating the rational ligands and optimizing synthetic conditions. The capping ligands of AuNCs played critical role in the synthesis of AuNCs, due to ligands on AuNCs maintaining their atomic precision and well-defined structure, stability and QY of AuNCs. The design of surface ligands is crucial for ligand-protected gold nanoclusters (Au NCs). Several sorts of ligands have been used to protect AuNCs, including thiolates [21,22], proteins [23], DNA [24], and some bio-small molecule such as amino acid [17]. Among these protecting ligands, biological small molecules are the most attractive, as bio-molecules ligands could provide a good protection for AuNCs and abundant surface groups owing to the strong bonding [25]. It is worth emphasizing that bio-molecules as a capping agent endows AuNCs with biocompatibilities, high stabilities, good water solubilities, and accessible surface modifications.

Herein, water-soluable high QY AuNCs with superior photobleaching resistance were reported by employing FA as capping ligand via a benign strategy (Scheme 1 ). The objectives of this work were to develop a novel ultra-stable AuNCs with the properties of strong luminescence, high QY, and ultra-long term stability. Though the similar FA-AuNCs have been reported by Yang et al. [26], and achieved the detection of Hg2+ and CH3Hg+ via dual fluorescent response mode, the FA-AuNCs synthesized in this work tried more mild synthesis, and obtained superior stability, and evaluated the sensing possibility of Hg2+ in cellular environment. The versatility and general utility of FA-AuNCs was testified in quantitative detection of Hg2+ and cellular imaging in real samples. Evidently, possessing excellent biocompatibility, the FA-AuNCs can also be utilized in cellular imaging and recognize the presence of Hg2+. This study provided a stable AuNCs and a more promising platform for accurately detecting of Hg2+ in environmental water and cells.
The chemicals and characterization were described in Supplementary Information in details.

The synthesis of FA-AuNCs was performed and optimized by varying the synthetic conditions including temperature, reaction time. HAuCl4 solution (2 mL, 30 mM) was poured in a round bottom flask containing 50 mL of ultrapure water with gentle stirring. Successively, saturated solution of FA (2 mL) was added dropwise to the HAuCl4 solution as the reaction temperature reached 70 °C and the pH was adjusted to 11 by 0.5 M NaOH solution simultaneously, and freshly prepared 1 mL of 0.1 M ascorbic acid (AA) solution was dripped slowly. The reaction mixture was kept at 70 °C for 26 h with stirring, a light-yellow color solution was obtained finally. To optimize the synthesis of FA-AuNCs, the parameter of reaction conditions such as reaction time (0.5–32 h) and temperature (30, 50, 70, 100 °C) were evaluated to determine the definitive condition by comparing their fluorescence. After centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 40 min, the product of FA-AuNCs was obtained by re-dispersing in equivalent ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C for further use.

To estimate the selectivity, FA-AuNCs were pipetted into different metal cation solutions (including: Pb2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Ag+, Cd2+ Ba2+ and Hg2+ (50 μM)) for incubating 5 min, then the FL spectra were recorded with excitation of 275 nm. The sensitivity was investigated by testing the FL response with various concentrations of Hg2+ (0–200 μM) samples by same process. All samples were tested three times to determine an average relative result.

Schematic synthetic illustration of FA-AuNCs (Scheme 1) demonstrated the FA-AuNCs obtained by employing ascorbic acid (AA) as reducing agents and FA as stabilizer, preserved the in-situ generation of Au (0) cores. It is worth emphasizing the role of the FA, as a capping ligand, with the functionalization of small molecules facilitating the formation and controlling the assembly of the AuNCs through aurophilic attraction abundant –COOH and –NH2 [27]. Besides providing precise protection for AuNCs, FA was also treated as the outermost surface layer of AuNCs to promote their water-solubility, and determining their physical and chemical properties [27]. The optimized condition for formation of the FA-AuNCs was evaluated by tuning the synthetic conditions (Fig. 1 and Fig. S1), the assay of fluorescent intensity of samples was acquired with temperature and reaction time from 30 °C, 50 °C, 70 °C and 100 °C crossed with 0.5–32 h systematically (The detailed results was shown in the supplementary information). The synthetic parameters including temperature and reaction time were estimated by combinatorial experimental exploration, the optimum condition was determined at 70 °C for 26 h according to FL intensity (Fig. 1). The pH in the synthesis of FA-AuNCs was set to 11, in which the carboxyl groups on FA would be deprotonated to carboxylate group [28]. These negative charged ligand shell could improve the accessibility of the FA protected AuNCs as ligand, would be spatially separated from each other due to the electrostatic repulsion between the carboxylate groups, allowing a better attainability of the gold core, which was conducive to the stability of FA-AuNCs [29].
The color of FA-AuNCs was light yellow with strong blue emission under UV-light (λ = 440 nm) (Scheme 1b). The brief benign synthesis process benefits to the utility of FA-AuNCs in sensing.

To investigate the morphology, chemical composition of FA-AuNCs, the FT-IR, XPS, and fluorescence spectra were performed and displayed in Fig. 2 . The FT-IR peaks at 2975 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1 corresponded to O–H stretching vibration in the carboxyl group, and 1640 cm−1 and 1606 cm−1 originated from bending vibration of N–H in primary amine. The sharp peaks at 874 and 843 cm−1 were assigned to the out-of-plane bending vibration of the N–H bond in the –NH2. Compared with FA, the feeble shift appeared in the FA-AuNCs might be caused by the bonding ways in the functional groups between FA and Au NCs, indicated rich interfacial functional groups facilitated the solubility of FA-AuNCs and promoted the ionic forces between the functional groups of ligands.

To further elucidate the chemical composition and surface modifications of FA-AuNCs, the valence states were detected by XPS analysis. FA-AuNCs were composed of C1s, N1s, Au4f and O1s, verifying their main components and surficial capping ligand (Fig. S3a). The typical C1s spectrum displayed peaks at 284.8, 285.9, 288.1 eV, corresponding to the C–C, C–N, and CO (Fig. S3b). The nitrogen peaks can be fitted into three peaks at 398.1, 399.2 and 401.0 eV, assigned to C–N–C, N–H, and C–NH2 [30]. The O 1s located at 530.7, 532.0, 533.3 and 534.5 eV corresponded to the CO, C–O, OC–O and COO−, respectively (Fig. S3d). The Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 spectra of FA-AuNCs can be identified with binding energies (BE) presenting at 83.8, 84.1, 87.0 and 87.6 eV (Fig. 2b), indicating the Au (III) had been reconstituted to two valence state (Au (0) and Au (I)) [31]. BE at 83.8 and 87.0 eV demonstrated the presence of the Au (0) [32,33]. Notably, the BE appeared at 84.1 and 87.6 eV, proving the presence of Au (I) in FA-AuNCs [34]. The BE peak mentioned above manifested the coexistence of Au (I) and Au (0), and the proportion of Au (I) was calculated to be 49.98%. The XPS surveys confirmed the existence of the functional groups such as –COOH and –NH2, indicating the formation of AuNCs with abundant surface groups.

FA-AuNCs exhibited pale yellow color with bright blue fluorescence under UV-light (inset of Fig. 2c), no obvious absorption can be observed manifested without obvious surface plasmon resonance, confirming ultra-small size of the FA-AuNCs [35]. A strong emission occurred at 440 nm excitated at 275 nm, representing a large Stoke shift of 165 nm, which beneficial to a low background requirement, interference of lighting scattering, applicable as a promising fluorescent probe for chemical sensing, and bio-imaging analysis [35]. The non-excitation dependence was proved by not shift with variation of excitation, indicated the uniform size of AuNCs (Fig. 2d–e) [36]. Time-resolved fluorescence decay of FA-AuNCs was evaluated and average fluorescence lifetime was determined at 3.66 ns (Fig. 2f). The superior QY was calculated to be 34.7% using quinine sulfate as standard [37], which might be caused by the electron-rich ligand FA contributed delocalized electrons to Au nucleus, enlarged the electron contribution ability and energy level of the frontier orbitals [38].

The pivotal stability of FA-AuNCs was evaluated by recording the FL spectra at different pH, ionic strength and storage time. The stability of synthesized FA-AuNCs was further unveiled by discussing the effect of pH on fluorescent intensity of FA-AuNCs. FL spectra of AuNCs maintained up to 90% in different pH solutions ranging from 2 to 9 (Fig. S4a), the result showed negligible effect on FA-AuNCs, indicating a wide range of resistance of pH. FA-AuNCs also exhibited high tolerance to high concentration of NaCl (0.5 M) (Fig. S4b), further provided supportive evidence of the excellent stability of FA-AuNCs. The photobleaching of FA-AuNCs was further studied, the FL intensity remained at 86% of the initial level after storage for 30 days and still steady at 80% after 200 days (Fig. S4c), almost 50% even for 8 years (Figs. S5–S6) [39], indicating strong antibleaching property of the FA-AuNCs. The superior photostability can be attributed to the protective and stabilizing effects of FA, which provided abundant hydrophilic groups, such as –COOH, and –NH2, supplied powerful ligands for stabilizing AuNCs with well-defined sizes and structures [27]. It is worth emphasizing that the FA not only afforded good water solubility, but also provided AuNCs with desirable sensing and imaging properties, high QYs and excellent photo-stability, which attributed to the compatible optimum synthetic conditions.

The TEM analysis shows uniform and monodisperse spherical morphology of FA-AuNCs (Fig. 3 a), and with a narrow diameter mean observed in particle size histogram was calculated to be 2.8 ± 0.4 nm (inset of Fig. 3a). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 3a) revealed the crystalline particles of FA-AuNCs, the well-resolved lattice spacing can be observed and calculated to 0.235 nm, resembling to the interplanar spacing of the (111) face-centered cubic of Au [40], indicating the formation of FA-AuNCs. The EDX analysis confirmed the existence of Au, Cu, C and Cl elements (Fig. 3b). Here, the C and Cu can be attributed to the carbon supported Cu grids, and the Cl was derived from the precursor HAuCl4. For further analysis, MALDI-TOF MS was employed to determine the molecular weight of FA-AuNCs, corresponding spectrum (Fig. 3c) in positive mode was obtained using sinapinic acid as matrix. The highest peaks at m/z of 619.309 was determined to be [Au4L2+3Na]3+, indicating the FA-AuNCs predominantly consist of 4 gold atoms. Moreover, the other observed peaks at 436.648, 701.324, 814.426 and 1134.112 revealed the existence of [Au4L+3Na]3+, [Au3L3+3Na]3+, [Au5L3+3Na]3+and [Au4L3+2Na]2+ in the FA-AuNCs. This finding confirmed the explicit core size of FA-AuNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and sensitivity of FA-AuNCs towards Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Superior FL stability and abundant surface groups endowed FA-AuNCs the possibility of utility in sensing and imaging. The sensing behavior was evaluated and displayed in Fig. 4 a, 50 μM of Hg2+ caused significant quenching fluorescence of FA-AuNCs within other relative ions. Compared with blank, FL intensity of Hg2++FA-AuNCs declined almost 86%, and the elimination under UV-light (Fig. 4c) can be noticed evidently. In comparison, other samples exhibited almost no change, and the corresponding images kept blue brightness comparative to the blank, indicating the good selectivity of FA-AuNCs for Hg2+. The competition experiments were supplied to elucidate the anti-interference of FA-AuNCs towards Hg2+. Most of ions exhibited no effect except Pb2+ and Cu2+ (Fig. 4b), but the interference of Pb2+ and Cu2+ can be ignored compared with Hg2+. Thus, high specificity of FA-AuNCs for Hg2+ was concluded, which can be explained by the metallophilic interaction between Au+ (4f145d10) and Hg2+ (4f145d10) [40]. A dispersion force endowed with high specificity and intension existed in the metal atoms of the closed shell layer, which can be amplified substantially by relative effects. This can be further echoed by the reported conclusion that a small amount of Au (I) was present on the surface of the Au core, as inferred from the XPS results. Hence, the high binding ability between Hg2+ and Au (I) determined a superior selectivity [41].

The sensitivity was estimated by recording different concentrations of Hg2+ with FA-AuNCs probe, and Fig. 5 a showed FL intensity gradually eliminated with increasing Hg2+ within 0–200 μM. Both fluorescence spectra and images demonstrated the FL intensity of FA-AuNCs was proportional to the concentration of Hg2+ tightly. The trend of fluorescence subduction of FA-AuNCs caused by increasing Hg2+ can be intuitively observed by the 3D waterfall diagram (Fig. 5c), with corresponding images (Fig. 5d–e). Fluorescence intensity ratio of I0/I showed a well linear fitting relationship with the Hg2+ concentration (I0 and I represent FL intensity of blank and with Hg2+). Noticeably, a good linear relationship (I0/I = 18.0x+1.03, R2 = 0.99) with the concentration of Hg2+ at 2.0–10.0 nM (Fig. 5b) was determined with LOD of 1.3 nM based on 3σ/k (σ represented the RSD and k was slope) [9]. The LOD was much lower than the maxium allowed Hg2+ in drinking water (10 nM) stipulated by the U.S. EPA [4], and was comparable even better than that listed reported work (Table S1), indicating the excellent sensing performance of FA-AuNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the feasibility of tracing the Hg2+ in environment, the analysis in river water was performed. The results manifested the recoveries ranging from 91.3% to 114.3% and RSD below 1.7% were obtained (Fig. 6 , Table S1), indicating the proposed FA-AuNCs probe was a precise and reliable approach for sensing Hg2+ in real water. The detection was further tested by ICP-OES synchronously, the results manifested the accuracy of detection were confirmed by ICP, which demonstrated the FA-AuNCs can be regarded as a promising practical probe for credible detection for Hg2+.

Possessing advantages of photostability and good solubility, the FA-AuNCs have potential utility in the bioimaging. The toxicity and biocompatibility was verified firstly by MTT method. Hence, cervical cancer HeLa cells were incubated in DMEM (Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium) with different concentrations of FA-AuNCs (0–184 μM) under standard conditions. The cell viability of the Hela cells incubated with FA-AuNCs for 24 h still remain more than 93% even the high concentration of FA-AuNCs (184 μM), proving the negligible toxicity of FA-AuNCs (Fig. S7). The cellular imaging and detection Hg2+ in vitro of FA-AuNCs was performed without any pretreatment due to the key role of FA in biomedical science [31]. Bright blue and green fluorescence appeared with FA-AuNCs incubated Hela cells for 2 h (Fig. 7 b) and analyzed using optical filter (Fig. 7e), manifesting excellent imaging ability of FA-AuNCs. To test the intracellular detection performance of FA-AuNCs, the 50 μM of Hg2+ was introduced in Hela cells. In comparison, the incubation of Hg2+ induced the precise fluorescence quenching, indicating the successfully tracing Hg2+ in living cells by FA-AuNCs, confirmed FA-AuNCs were capable of intracellular detecting Hg2+ (Fig. 7c). Above results manifested low toxic FA-AuNCs were promising for the detection of exogenous and endogenous Hg2+ in live cells.

The sensing mechanism for FA-AuNCs tracing Hg2+ should be investigated comprehensively. The UV–Vis absorption shown that Hg2+ had a strong absorption at 310 nm (Fig. 8 a), which overlapped well with the excitation spectra of the FA-AuNCs, suggesting the quenching fluorescence induced by the Hg2+ may be caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filtering effect (IFE) [42]. However, the fluorescence lifetime of the FA-AuNCs remained almost invariable (3.65 ns) with Hg2+ (Fig. 8b), no significant change of lifetime proved the mechanism of IFE not FRET [43]. Also, the static quenching effect (SQE) rather than a dynamic quenching effect (DQE) can be proved by the negligible decay with Hg2+. The IFE generally referred to the absorption of the excitation or emission spectra of fluorophores by light-absorbing substances, caused the quenching fluorescence [44]. It had been widely favorable in the design of chemo- and bio-sensors due to its flexibility and substantially enhanced sensitivity and selectivity [35]. In addition, the primary and secondary IFE corresponded to the absorption of excitation radiation by various chromophores and emission radiation by diverse chromophores. The Stern-Volmer equation had been analyzed as follows: I0/I = 1+Ksv·[Q] = 1+Kq·τ0[Q]. Ksv corresponded to the quenching constant, τ0 attaches to the lifetime (3.66 ns), Kq was the quenching rate constant and [Q] represented the concentration of Hg2+. According to the linear equation (I0/I = 18.0x+1.03, R2 = 0.99), the Stern-Volmer constant Ksv was acquired as 1.8 × 107 L−1mol−1. And Kq was 5.14 × 1015 L mol−1 s−1 was calculated by Kq = Ksv/τ0, which was bigger than the maximum diffusion coefficient of 2 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1, further verifying the SQE mechanism in the detection process of FA-AuNCs [45].

In this work, the FA-AuNCs with high QY and superior stability was synthesized and employed as fluorescent probe for detecting Hg2+ in real water and Hela cells. The excellent photostability of FA-AuNCs were verified, which promoted their promising utility in detection and bioimaging. The FA-AuNCs achieved fluorescent detecting Hg2+ with limit of 1.3 nM, reached the monitoring level in drink water. Remarkably, the detection can be realized in the Hela cells, reveals a novel and facile approach for intracellular sensing Hg2+. With virtue of high QY, prominent photostability, low toxicity and good cytocompatibility, FA-AuNCs were expected to become a promising nanoprobe and cell imaging material. Though possessing above merits, the high costs and non-portability became inevitable defects in the application of FA-AuNCs. The efforts will continue strive to promote their advantages, and solve their portability and high costs issues, to meet the future sustainable applications.

Ruyan Xie: Experimental design, Experimental implementation, Data organization, First draft of manuscript writing, Manuscript revision. Dongyue Su*: Experimental design, Data organization, Experimental data proofreading. Ying Song: Experimental data proofreading. Peng Sun: Figures editing, Data organization. Baodong Mao*: Data organization, Manuscript revision. Miaomiao Tian: Resources. Fang Chai*: Project proposal design, experiment idea guidance, manuscript writing guidance, Writing-review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge support from NNSF of China (22171060), Harbin Normal University (HSDSSCX2022-14).
